abstract:
While maternal mortality has long been a focal point of global health, maternal morbidity remains a significantly neglected dimension of reproductive health in Kenya and across much of sub-Saharan Africa. This study addresses the data gap by analyzing the prevalence and determinants of self-reported maternal complications in Kenya, utilizing the 1998 Demographic and Health Survey (DHS)—the only national survey in the country to have collected such specific data. The article provides a critical evaluation of current methodologies used to measure maternal morbidity in developing regions, highlighting their respective strengths and inherent limitations, such as recall and selection biases. 

The findings reveal that over half of Kenyan women experienced at least one complication during childbirth, with prolonged labor emerging as the most frequently reported issue (34.5%), followed by excessive hemorrhage (23.3%). Regional disparities are particularly pronounced for hemorrhage, sepsis, and eclampsia. Statistical modeling indicates that reports of morbidity are significantly associated with higher parity, the presence of a skilled birth attendant, ethnicity, and province of residence. The study concludes that, in the absence of robust vital registration systems, self-recorded data from population-based surveys remains the most viable tool for identifying maternal health priorities and assessing the quality of obstetric care in sub-Saharan Africa.
